prompt: Forcing is a technique in set theory used primarily to:
answer: Prove that certain statements are independent of ZFC by constructing generic extensions of models.
choices: Prove that certain statements are independent of ZFC by constructing generic extensions of models.
Prove the consistency of ZF from Peano arithmetic.
Construct explicit well-orderings of the reals.
Show that all axioms of ZFC are theorems of each other.
explanation: Step 1: Starting from a model M of ZFC, one adjoins a generic object G via a partial order (poset) P in M. Step 2: The extension M[G] is a larger model of ZFC in which new sets exist and old statements may become false. Final answer: Prove that certain statements are independent of ZFC by constructing generic extensions of models.

prompt: The key idea of forcing is that one forces a statement to be true in the extension by:
answer: Showing that there is a dense set of conditions that decide the statement positively, so the generic filter must meet that set.
choices: Showing that there is a dense set of conditions that decide the statement positively, so the generic filter must meet that set.
Adding the statement as a new axiom by hand.
Collapsing all cardinals below it.
Using only the constructible universe L.
explanation: Step 1: A condition p 'forces' a statement $\phi$ (written p $\Vdash$ $\phi$) if every generic filter containing p yields an extension in which $\phi$ holds. Step 2: If the set of conditions forcing $\phi$ is dense, the generic G will contain one of them. Final answer: Showing that there is a dense set of conditions that decide the statement positively, so the generic filter must meet that set.

prompt: In forcing, a *poset* (partial order) P is used to:
answer: Describe the possible 'approximations' or 'conditions' that will be used to build the generic object G.
choices: Describe the possible 'approximations' or 'conditions' that will be used to build the generic object G.
Well-order the ground model.
Construct the power set of the empty set.
Prove the axiom of extensionality.
explanation: Step 1: Elements of P are 'pieces of information' about the object being added (e.g., finite pieces of a function, or finite approximations to a real). Step 2: Stronger conditions (larger in the order) give more information. Final answer: Describe the possible 'approximations' or 'conditions' that will be used to build the generic object G.

prompt: A filter G on the poset P is *generic* over the model M if:
answer: G intersects every dense subset of P that belongs to M.
choices: G intersects every dense subset of P that belongs to M.
G is an ultrafilter on the Boolean completion of P.
G is countable in the extension.
G contains only the maximal element of P.
explanation: Step 1: 'Dense' means that below any condition there is a stronger condition in the set. Step 2: Genericity ensures that G 'meets all the requirements' that M can express, so that the extension M[G] sees all the dense sets from M as being hit. Final answer: G intersects every dense subset of P that belongs to M.

prompt: Cohen used forcing to show the independence of the continuum hypothesis by:
answer: Adding many new reals (Cohen reals) so that in the extension $2^{\aleph_0}$ becomes larger than $\aleph_1$ while the ground model cardinals are preserved.
choices: Adding many new reals (Cohen reals) so that in the extension $2^{\aleph_0}$ becomes larger than $\aleph_1$ while the ground model cardinals are preserved.
Collapsing $\aleph_1$ to be countable.
Making all sets constructible.
Adding a measurable cardinal.
explanation: Step 1: The poset of finite partial functions from $\omega$ to 2 (or finite conditions for adding a real) is used; a generic adds a new real. Step 2: Iterated or product forcing adds many reals, making the continuum large while not collapsing cardinals if the poset is ccc. Final answer: Adding many new reals (Cohen reals) so that in the extension $2^{\aleph_0}$ becomes larger than $\aleph_1$ while the ground model cardinals are preserved.

prompt: To show that AC is independent of ZF, one typically uses:
answer: Symmetric extensions or Fraenkel-Mostowski permutation models (or forcing with atoms) in which a set of reals or a Dedekind-finite infinite set exists that cannot be well-ordered.
choices: Symmetric extensions or Fraenkel-Mostowski permutation models (or forcing with atoms) in which a set of reals or a Dedekind-finite infinite set exists that cannot be well-ordered.
Cohen forcing over L.
The constructible universe L.
Large cardinal axioms.
explanation: Step 1: In permutation models one has a group of permutations of atoms acting on the universe; only symmetric names are allowed in the model. Step 2: One can arrange that a countable collection of pairs of atoms has no choice function in the model. Final answer: Symmetric extensions or Fraenkel-Mostowski permutation models (or forcing with atoms) in which a set of reals or a Dedekind-finite infinite set exists that cannot be well-ordered.

prompt: A poset P is said to satisfy the countable chain condition (ccc) if:
answer: Every antichain in P is countable. This implies that P preserves all cardinals and cofinalities (in particular does not collapse $\aleph_1$).
choices: Every antichain in P is countable. This implies that P preserves all cardinals and cofinalities (in particular does not collapse $\aleph_1$).
P is countable.
P has no maximal element.
P collapses all cardinals above $\aleph_1$.
explanation: Step 1: An antichain is a set of pairwise incompatible conditions. Step 2: By a delta-system or pressing-down argument, ccc posets do not add new countable sequences of ground model ordinals, hence preserve $\aleph_1$ and all larger cardinals. Final answer: Every antichain in P is countable. This implies that P preserves all cardinals and cofinalities (in particular does not collapse $\aleph_1$).

prompt: The forcing relation $p \Vdash \phi$ is defined in the ground model M and has the key property that:
answer: It is definable in M, and the truth of $\phi$ in M[G] is completely determined by which conditions in G force $\phi$ or its negation.
choices: It is definable in M, and the truth of $\phi$ in M[G] is completely determined by which conditions in G force $\phi$ or its negation.
It requires the generic G to be already present in M.
It only works for atomic formulas.
It is the same as the satisfaction relation in M.
explanation: Step 1: The definition of $\Vdash$ is by induction on formula complexity and is internal to M (using only the poset that lives in M). Step 2: For any statement $\phi$, exactly one of $\phi$ or $\neg\phi$ will be forced by some condition in the generic G; hence M[G] $\models \phi$ or not is decided inside M. Final answer: It is definable in M, and the truth of $\phi$ in M[G] is completely determined by which conditions in G force $\phi$ or its negation.
